metadata.title: Are the answers all out there? Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucratic responsiveness

abstract: Under freedom of information (FOI) regulations, public officials are pressured to grant citizens access to public information by responding to citizen information requests. However, despite FOI regulations, information requests are treated with varying bureaucratic attention, resulting in a high number of ignored or overdue requests by public organizations. Focusing on this bureaucratic discrimination, this study aims to explain the determinants of varying bureaucratic responsiveness to citizen information requests. Responsiveness in this case is therefore either a successful response to the request, a refusal to respond to the request, or no response at all (i.e., the request is ignored). By drawing on public accountability, and thus a citizen-driven model of bureaucratic responsiveness, we shed light on the human aspect behind responsiveness to information requests. This research argues that the request's topic as an accountability-seeking indicator and the communication tone as an indicator for underlying emotions influence responsiveness. The results from applying text mining and text analysis techniques, such as topic modeling and sentiment analysis, on over 100,000 citizen information requests filed via a German online FOI platform support these assumptions.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: Growing dissatisfaction with official secrecy and increasing public outcry for transparent information provision has urged governments and policy-makers worldwide to issue freedom of information (FOI) regulations (e.g., Michener, 2011;Worthy, 2010). FOI acts enable citizens to hold public bodies accountable for their actions by providing a legal basis for demanding government information. By the same token, being obliged to disclose information implies a high level of bureaucratic responsiveness to citizens' information demand (Berliner, 2014;Pozen, 2017). To facilitate information disclosure, third parties such as non-governmental organizations have implemented online platforms and moderate information exchange between citizens and the government-meaning citizens' requests for information from a public organization and the corresponding response from the organization (e.g., Ansell & Miura, 2020;Attard et al., 2015;Carter & Stratton, 2021;Nikiforova & Lnenicka, 2021).

In the context of this information exchange between a citizen and a public organization, bureaucratic responsiveness is defined as a public organization's response to citizens' information requests. We distinguish between three types of responses: the public organization provides the information requested by citizens; it does not share the demanded information but provides an answer to the request; the organization ignores the request and does not provide an answer. Previous research has found that requests receive different bureaucratic attention (Balla, 2000;Fox et al., 2011), meaning that there is discrimination in the administrative processing of information requests.

Apart from exogenous factors, such as spatial location, ruling governing parties or the degree of legal enforcement (Berliner et al., 2021;Fox et al., 2011;Poole, 2019), discrimination could originate from the specific purpose and content of requests (Almanzar et al., 2018;Bagozzi et al., 2021). On that account, different topics of information requests can be assigned to different levels of public accountability and public relevance. A request may involve an issue of high public accountability with the potential to generate high public salience, or it may involve an individual, private issue of less public relevance (Berliner et al., 2018;Michener & Worthy, 2018). In addition, not only the topic, but also the way in which requests are formulated can affect responsiveness (Cherry & McMenemy, 2013;Cuillier, 2010;Epstein et al., 2021). Especially in online interaction and digital discourse, the tone and style of a message have the power to polarize and shape the direction of communication (Anderson et al., 2014;Tromble, 2018). Underlying positive or negative emotions stemming from the wording and tone of the request may explain differences in bureaucratic responsiveness.

This study builds on research on public accountability and emotions in requesting government information. Under the premise of a human-oriented view of bureaucratic responsiveness, the study aims to shed light on the specific demand for public information and to draw attention to triggers of responsiveness. It asks to what extent the information request's topic and communication tone impact the likelihood of receiving a (positive) response from a public organization. To answer our research question, we analyzed over 100,000 citizen information requests submitted to a German online FOI platform between 2011 and 2021, and the corresponding responses from public organizations.

The contribution of this article is threefold. First, it adds to literature on government transparency by analyzing how open public organizations are to citizens' requests for information disclosure. It thus extends previous research that has focused on the drivers of government openness (e.g., Tavares & da Cruz, 2020;Wang & Lo, 2016;Grimmelikhuijsen & Feeney, 2017) by investigating the interplay between the demand for and supply of public information. Second, this 14680491, 0, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/gove.12805 by Johannes Kepler Universität Linz, Wiley Online Library on [14/06/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License study contributes to research on citizen-state interaction and bureaucratic responsiveness (e.g., Alkadry, 2003;Bryer, 2009;Liao, 2018;Rourke, 1969;Saltzstein, 1985Saltzstein, , 1992;;Verba & Nie, 1972;Yang & Callahan, 2007;Yang & Pandey, 2007) by investigating the link between characteristics of citizen information requests and the corresponding public organizations' responses. Third, it advances the literature on FOI by focusing on the topic of the requests submitted to a German FOI platform, whereas similar studies tend to examine FOI requests in transitional democracies (e.g., Bagozzi et al., 2021;Berliner et al., 2021;Fox et al., 2011;Lagunes & Pocasangre, 2019;Su & Meng, 2016). We thus highlight information demand and overall likelihood of responsiveness in a more mature democracy, thereby responding to prior limitations (Holsen & Pasquier, 2012) and calls for further research (Berliner et al., 2018).

The remainder of this article is structured as follows. First, from a brief theoretical overview, two assumptions about the determinants of the response behavior of public organizations are derived with a focus on the German research context. Second, the data and the platform are described. Next, text analysis techniques such as topic modeling via Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and sentiment analysis are applied to analyze the information requests of German citizens. Third, a descriptive overview and the results of a multinomial logistic regression model show the effects of requests' characteristics on bureaucratic responsiveness. The article concludes with a discussion of the findings, limitations and suggestions for further research.

Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: Germany is a federal state, and responsibilities are divided between the central government and the 16 states ('Länder'). The varying jurisdictions and differences in legal regulations lead to high fragmentation and coordination challenges, making bureaucracy complex and oftentimes difficult to comprehend (Bach & Jann, 2010;Bogumil & Jann, 2020). Consequently, depending on the public organization's jurisdiction, different FOI regulations must be applied as the legal basis for responding to information requests. While the German federal FOI law, which became effective in 2006, covers federal public bodies, it does not apply to state and municipal authorities (Mueller et al., 2019). Until 2021, 13 out of the 16 German states have passed FOI regulations.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: In a seminal work, Saltzstein (1985, p. 283) defines bureaucratic responsiveness as "a crucial component of the overall responsiveness of the political system". Accountability, transparency and responsiveness are major underlying factors for state institutions to be considered stable, functioning (Roberts, 2006) and trustworthy (Grimmelikhuijsen & Knies, 2017). According to Verba and Nie (1972, p. 300), democratic accountability and responsiveness represent a form of interaction "between citizens and government, one in which the citizen articulates certain preferences and/or applies pressure on the government and the government in turn-if it is responsive-attempts to satisfy these preferences". A citizen-centered model has evoked from these prior definitions, stating that "public administrators should fulfill their democratic responsibilities by faithfully carrying out citizen demands" (Liao, 2018, p.160). Drawing from this, many scholars conceptualized bureaucratic responsiveness from a micro-perspective as a part of citizen-state interaction (Alkadry, 2003;Balla, 2000;Epstein et al., 2021;Liao, 2018;Saltzstein, 1992;Yang & Callahan, 2007).

In light of this interaction and the principles of mutual checks and balances (Sartori, 1987), being heard and being granted adequate access to information permits citizens to exert control, and thus, hold government or public bodies accountable for their actions (Camaj, 2016). FOI laws and increasing pressure for information disclosure put public officials in a situation where they are expected to be responsive and to provide answers to citizens' inquiries. This specific form of bureaucratic responsiveness has been investigated by analyzing public organizations' responsiveness to information requests (Bagozzi et al., 2021;Fox et al., 2011), thereby describing the interplay between the supply and demand of public information.

Whereas previous research has explained varying levels of responsiveness by external conditions, such as the political landscape or the institutional culture in which the organization operates (Berliner et al., 2021;Poole, 2019;Su & Meng, 2016), this study argues that a public organization's likelihood of being responsive to information requests can be explained by individual human behavior. It is the street-level bureaucrats, who communicate directly with the citizens and are responsible for answering requests. Although these bureaucrats are bound by laws and administrative regulations, they have a certain degree of leeway in discretion and freedom in how they enforce the rules (Lipsky, 2010). Sometimes bureaucrats are also simply unwilling to respond for various reasons, and ignore the reception of information requests (Alkadry, 2003;Fox et al., 2011). Berliner et al. (2021, p. 7) further propose that "bureaucrats may be discretionarily unresponsive out of an interest in minimizing workloads, particularly in understaffed agencies".

Moreover, requesters' identity plays a significant role, as institutional requesters (Michener et al., 2020) or requesters with self-reported status and knowledge (Rodríguez & Rossel, 2018) receive significantly more bureaucratic attention when submitting online information requests. These individual triggers of responsiveness shed light on a certain degree of street-level decision-making and administrative discrimination in responding to information requests. Bureaucratic responsiveness to citizen requests thus depends not only on official FOI regulations or exogenous effects, but also on the individual bureaucrats responsible for processing the information request and the decisions they make at the front line.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: Information requests cover a wide range of different topics. Michener and Worthy (2018) distinguish between publicly and privately oriented information requests. First, publicly oriented information requests consist of "other-regarding" topics (Michener & Worthy, 2018, p. 485) meaning that the information is relevant to society as a whole. This implies that citizens seek to hold government accountable by asking topics of high public relevance. Berliner et al. (2018: 226) define information that is highly salient and relevant to public accountability "as that which is useful in evaluating government performance, decision-making, or detecting malfeasance". Pursuant to this definition, particularly environmental issues, budget and spending, recruitment of government employees, finance and taxes, medical supplies and corruption in public procurement are considered to be issues of high public accountability (Berliner et al., 2018).

Second, privately oriented information requests deal with "self-regarding" interests that are primarily relevant to the individual requester (Michener & Worthy, 2018, p. 485). Accordingly, they seek to obtain access to very specific information by submitting selective, individual requests on more private matters (Berliner et al., 2018(Berliner et al., , 2021;;Michener & Worthy, 2018;Su & Meng, 2016). Requests about individual needs, such as access to social benefits, guidance on bureaucratic procedures or other very specific, personal interests, contribute to holding government accountable to a very limited extent (Berliner et al., 2018). These are considered topics of low public accountability, and thus, of low salience.

By the same token, public officials' incentives for being responsive vary with regard to distinctive topics of requests (Bagozzi et al., 2021). As demand for information on high accountability-seeking topics has the potential to attract public attention, officials may feel a stronger urge to respond due to outside pressure (Busuioc & Lodge, 2016). In such cases, ignoring the request would prevent citizens from obtaining important information or from using a service they are entitled to (Epstein et al., 2021), and thus, create public salience. Not only could it give the impression that the organization has something to hide, but also that officials are lazy or dilatory if requests are ignored. As a result, the reputation of the responsible public organization could suffer. The underlying link between officials' reputation and accountability is defined as one being dependent on the audience because "reputation focuses organizational attention and equips organizations with a rationale for prioritizing among multiple external accountability demands" (Busuioc & Lodge, 2016, p. 255).

Particularly in Germany such a prioritization could be strong as there is a distinct culture of professionalism. Public officials are expected to have a high level of expertize and knowledge (Jann & Veit, 2015), so there might be a strong reluctance among them to engage in activities that may reflect poorly on their department or agency. Indeed, being responsive to high accountability-seeking requests can positively stress officials' service quality and even demonstrate "governing capacities" (Su & Meng, 2016, p. 55). To prevent potential accusations or negative public attention, officials may prioritize providing information to requests of high accountability over requests about topics of low accountability.

Nevertheless, there is some controversy about the responsiveness that highly salient, publicly relevant and sensitive topics receive. For example, requests about state inefficiencies, power abuses, security failures or corruption are more likely to be refused (Bagozzi et al., 2021) or not properly responded to (Almanzar et al., 2018), despite being classified as very important for the public (Berliner et al., 2018). Su and Meng (2016, p. 55) argue that "governments, as well as their agents, are not tools but rational actors with their own interests and autonomy", meaning that public officials weigh their own benefits when responding to information requests. In doing so, public officials may also consider incentives for ignoring requests.

In addition, laws and regulations, especially on FOI, can be complicated and difficult to interpret, often leaving public officials unsure of what information is required by law to be released, what is protected by exemptional clauses and how to properly respond in these cases (Taylor & Burt, 2010). Particularly in Germany, which has a complex legal and bureaucratic system (Bach & Jann, 2010) and strong privacy laws that protect the rights of citizens, it can be difficult for public officials to assess what type of information can be released. Therefore, not answering high accountability-seeking requests could spare them potential personal repercussions that might arise in case they accidently reveal false, excessive, or proprietary information. To avoid personal consequences, the most appealing option for officials in such cases might be ignoring requests at all.

Drawing from this argumentation, public officials face a certain trade-off in their responsiveness behavior. Despite the existence of FOI laws, information disclosure and bureaucratic responsiveness to information demands are not as clear and distinct as one might assume. While providing too little information might result in public attention, providing too much information could result in intra-organizational attention leading to possible sanctions. Considering the German research context and principles of accountability (Berliner et al., 2018, 2021, Busuioc & Lodge, 2016), we hypothesize: Hypothesis 1. Information requests containing a high-accountability seeking topic are more likely to receive a response.

Next to the topic, a crucial attribute of interpersonal interaction is the tone of communication, which arises from the underlying emotions of the speaker. Liebenthal et al. (2016, p. 1) point out that, for example, "the meaning of the word "mad," whether spoken in the sense of "mentally disturbed," "furious," or "wildly excited," can be disambiguated based on evaluation of contextual paralinguistic information such as the speaker's current emotional status, as disclosed by their voice tone […]". In contrast to spoken conversation, emotional status can be more difficult to identify in written communication. Particularly in online communication, style, expression, and the specific word usage play a significant role in determining the underlying emotions, and, hence, how a message is received and how the recipient responds to it (Stieglitz & Dang-Xuan, 2013;Zavattaro et al., 2015). Accordingly, the way in which a message is framed can affect its treatment meaning that there is a reciprocity in the tone of written communication. An angry tone or negative words in a question may lead to an even angrier tone in the response or to no response at all (see also Anderson et al., 2014;Tromble, 2018).

In the context of information exchange, researchers have already investigated the important role of emotions and found that a person's mood substantially impacts information sharing (and seeking) behavior (Dardenne et al., 2011;Peters et al., 2009). Applying this idea of reciprocity to the interaction between citizens and government, Epstein et al. (2021) describe the customer-related and service-oriented role in which public officials find themselves. In the course of their duty of representing the state and of ensuring a positive service quality evaluation, they are required to manage and control their emotions and expressions to deliver satisfying public services. When interacting with citizens, they thus must retain a friendly, cooperative attitude. Under this premise, all requests should be treated equally, and less positive, less polite, or less eloquent requests should not be neglected. However, according to Epstein et al. (2021, p. 7f), "the people tasked with fielding and responding to citizen requests for information are individuals who may be balancing multiple modes of communication […] and who likely experience the full range of emotions" when being exposed to all kinds of information requests.

Consequently, since the exchange of government information and the processing of citizen requests is a form of human interaction, emotions are involved. A request that is formulated in a friendly way with positive words could highlight positive underlying emotions. For example, Cuillier (2010) finds that threatening letter tones in information requests were more effective in eliciting a timely response than friendly letter tones asking about the same topic, but public officials provided more detailed and more helpful responses to the friendly requests. In that sense, public officials may be more likely to respond to online requests that include positive words and tone. Therefore, on the grounds of principles of reciprocal interaction, we argue that the underlying emotions of a request, expressed through the communication tone, also affect the officials' emotions and thus the bureaucratic responsiveness to the request. We hypothesize the following:
Hypothesis 2. Information requests containing a positive communication tone are more likely to receive a response.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: FOI platforms facilitate citizen-state interaction by enabling citizens to ask questions and directly forwarding the request to the responsible public organization. Research on FOI has shifted toward examining interaction through information requests posted on platforms (Carter & Stratton, 2021), as data stemming from FOI platforms have proven useful for analyzing bureaucratic response patterns. The underlying data for this study are publicly available and stem from the German FOI platform 'FragdenStaat' ('AskTheState'), a non-profit project of the Open Knowledge Foundation Germany. The platform operates under the paramount goal of promoting transparency and aims at facilitating information exchange between the public and authorities, thus acting as an intermediary. According to the platform, to date more than half of all German information requests are submitted via the platform (FragdenStaat, 2023).

To post a request, platform users must create an account. However, requesting is anonymous, meaning that requests cannot be traced back to the requester's identity, unless the requester provides personal details in the message itself. Subsequently, the platform providers categorize and process the request, add supplementary information about the legislation under which the request falls and then forward it to the addressed organization on behalf of the requester. Each request is given an automatically generated due date, indicating that information has to be provided within a timespan of 1 month. If no response is received within 6 months after the request has been posted, the request is classified as 'asleep' by the FOI platform operators. The requesters are informed of this process and given the possibility to 'wake up' the request if they so wish.

We downloaded all the requests and corresponding metadata from the FOI platform in October 2021 and imported them into a statistical data analysis program. Each request is identified through a unique identification number (ID number). After eliminating duplicates, 1 the dataset consisted of 176,595 unique requests submitted over a period of 10.5 years, between spring 2011 and fall 2021, to 8086 different German public bodies. On average, more than 10,000 information requests are posted on the platform per year. However, a large portion of the requests were posted in 2019. Apart from increasing public awareness of the platform, the increase in usage over time can be explained by the platform's launch of specific campaigns. For one, a big campaign intended to detect violations against hygiene regulations and consumer protection (55,792 requests). A second campaign intended to fight against copyright regulations regarding public documents, following the publication of a report about the herbicide glyphosate (44,657 requests). Contrary to requests about hygiene regulations and consumer protection, the requests about the glyphosate document were all forwarded to the same public body within a very short time frame (April to June) in 2019 and received the same standard answer, which is why including these requests in our analysis would severely bias the results. We therefore decided to exclude these 44,657 requests from the following analysis.

Upon receipt and throughout the entire process, requests are assigned a status indicating their stage in the process. This status can either be 'resolved', 'asleep or overdue' and 'awaiting response, classification, or confirmation'. Being assigned the status 'resolved' does not indicate whether the request was successful or refused. It simply indicates that the request received a response from a public body. Of our sample of 131,938 requests, 71,760 are resolved, 45,142 are overdue, and 15,036 are awaiting further action. Resolved requests are then further distinguished into whether they have been successfully resolved (34,619), partially successfully resolved (6,138), withdrawn by the requester (9,987), refused or no information could be provided (18,483). For the remaining 1.92% ( 2533) of all resolved requests, this further distinction was missing in the data. After elimination of all pending and withdrawn requests, as well as other missing values, the final sample consists of 102,707 observations, that is, individual information requests, sent to 7339 public organizations.

sections.preview_head: FOI platforms facilitate citizen-state interaction by enabling citizens to ask questions and directly forwarding the request to the responsible public organization. Research on FOI has shifted toward examining interaction through information requests posted on platforms (Carter & Stratton, 2021), as data stemming from FOI platforms have proven useful for analyzing bureaucratic response patterns. The underlying data for this study are publicly available and stem from the German FOI platform 'FragdenStaat' ('AskTheState'), a non-profit project of the Open Knowledge Foundation Germany.
sections.preview_tail: Resolved requests are then further distinguished into whether they have been successfully resolved (34,619), partially successfully resolved (6,138), withdrawn by the requester (9,987), refused or no information could be provided (18,483). For the remaining 1.92% ( 2533) of all resolved requests, this further distinction was missing in the data. After elimination of all pending and withdrawn requests, as well as other missing values, the final sample consists of 102,707 observations, that is, individual information requests, sent to 7339 public organizations.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: Dependent variable. Bureaucratic responsiveness is aggregated and measured by three categories indicating how the concerned authority responded to the request (see also Su & Meng, 2016) ('3' = resolved successfully or partially successful, '2' = refused or no information could be given, '1' = no response at all ('asleep or overdue', reference category)).

Independent variables. To operationalize the request's topic and thus explain the kind of information citizens are interested in, we proceeded as follows. First, a large number of requests were made in the context of the hygiene regulation campaign. The topic of these requests is therefore clear and does not require further specification. Second, to generate the overarching topics from all the remaining 76,146 requests, we conduct text analysis and topic modeling of the individual request text. The full analysis is provided in Appendix A in Supporting Information S1. The resulting 10 topic categories together with the hygiene regulation campaign topic are treated as categorical variable in our model. In addition to the request's topic, we summarize the topics according to their accountability-seeking potential by establishing categories ('low poten tial' = 1, 'medium potential' = '2', 'medium/high potential' = '3', 'high potential' = '4'). The procedure is described in Appendix A in Supporting Information S1.

The second independent variable is the communication tone. We define the tone as an expression of the underlying sentiment originating from the words used in the request. Therefore, sentiment analysis is used to analyze the underlying emotions of requesters. Through this process, each word in a request is ascribed to a continuous numeric value representing the degree of 'positive' emotion. A higher value means that more words with positive sentiment association have been used in the request. Thus, the higher the value the 'more positive' the requester's communication tone. The detailed procedure is explained in Appendix B in Supporting Information S1, along with examples of a positive and a negative request.

Control variables. The strength of the FOI law in force is included in the model, accounting for the jurisdiction under which the corresponding public organization operates. Requests could either fall under no own FOI regulation ('0' = Bavaria, Lower Saxony, Saxony), under the federal FOI act ('1'), under a specific state FOI act ('2' = Baden Wurttemberg, Berlin, Brandenburg, Hessen, Mecklenburg-West Pomerania, North Rhine Westphalia, Saarland, Saxony Anhalt, Schleswig-Holstein), under a transparency law ('3' = Bremen, Hamburg, Rhineland Palatinate, Thuringia), or under EU jurisdiction ('4').

To account for additional style-related effects and to measure text complexity, the length of the request in terms of the logged number of words used was included as a control variable (Berliner et al., 2021). Lastly, the time in which the request had been filed needs to be considered. We thus included a categorical control variable for the year of the posting of the request.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: Two multinomial logistic regression models are run to predict three potential outcome categories of bureaucratic responsiveness (Agresti, 2013;Bayaga, 2010;Harrell, 2015). We use the 'mblogit' function from the R package 'mclogit' (Elff, 2021). This allows to examine how the different predictors affect the odds of falling into a response category relative to the reference 14680491, 0, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/gove.12805 by Johannes Kepler Universität Linz, Wiley Online Library on [14/06/2023]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License category ('asleep or overdue'). To establish a truly topic-driven responsiveness prediction we include random effects for each public organization (7339 in total). Random effects account for the effects each public organization on its own could have originating from the organization's individual disclosure incentives. Such incentives differ regarding the varying institutional, structural, organizational or foundational motivations of organizations operating in the public realm (e.g., Berliner et al., 2021;Michener et al., 2021).

The first model tests the effect of the request's topic (Model I), and the second model validates the effect of the topics' accountability-seeking potential (Model II). Therefore, we test the hypotheses by using two models. Moreover, we conduct an exploratory analysis of the interplay between topic and communication tone including interaction terms of the two independent variables (Baron & Kenny, 1986). In addition, a simplified binary logistic model was run with only two outcome possibilities (resolved = either 'successful', 'partially successful' or 'refused' (1); and ignored = 'asleep or overdue' (0)). Finally, to control for overall robustness and deviations in findings, we run two supplementary models investigating this binary outcome variable by applying linear regression. First, we use the 'felm' function of the R package 'lfe' to account for public organization fixed effects (instead of random effects). Second, we run a multilevel model using public organizations as a grouping variable. These two models are available from the authors on request.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: First, we examine descriptive statistics of all numeric predictors and the frequency distributions of the categorical predictors. Table 1 provides the crosstabulation along with the mean values of the independent variables across the outcome categories. In Appendix C in Supporting Information S1, a frequency distribution of the number of requests over the years and across topics is provided.

Statistical analysis of the crosstabulation yields a significant chi-square test of independence (χ 2 = 16,721.29, p < 0.000), rejecting the null hypothesis and outlining an underlying relationship between the categorical topic variables. Overall, the frequency distributions reveal a first hint toward substantiating a topic-responsiveness contingency. While requests about education and public safety and security were mostly (at least partially) successful in relative terms (over 60% of requests about topic 3 and topic 4), the opposite is true for requests about hygiene regulations and consumer protection, of which over 60% remained unanswered (topic 11).

A different picture emerges for the communication tone. The mean values are stable across the predicted variable's categories and close to zero. However, when looking at the distinct ranges between the minimum and maximum values across the predicted categories, some variation becomes apparent. To further investigate differences in communication tone across categories, a one-way ANOVA model (F = 53.32, p < 0.000) was run, confirming within-group differences in communication tone.

Second, the two regression models with exponentiations of the base log-odds coefficients (odd ratios) for all predictors are provided in Table 2. Analyses of the different topics' odds ratio patterns and their values, not only within a predicted category, but also across predicted categories, outline an association between the request's topic and responsiveness (p values below the 5% threshold) in Model I. This association is confirmed by the significant relationship between accountability-seeking potential and responsiveness in Model II. Requests about topics of higher ascribed public relevance have relatively high odds of receiving a response from the organization addressed, regardless of whether the response is a refusal or a successful information provision.

In comparison, the odds of a request about, for example, hygiene regulations (topic 11, low accountability-seeking in model II) being successfully answered, rather than ignored, are 0.46 times the odds of a request about the reference topic, meaning that these requests are more likely to be ignored than answered. Moreover, for example, an information request that contains an inquiry about topic four is 1.09 times more likely to be successfully answered than an information request about topic 9 (medium accountability-seeking in model II).

The same interpretation can be applied for the refused requests. Again, a pattern of increasing odds ratios accompanying higher accountability potential was observed, although in this case the response is a refusal to provide information. Apart from the low accountability-seeking topic of hygiene regulations, all topics have higher odds of being successfully answered or refused rather than of not being answered at all (odds ratio all higher than 1). This shows that the reference category represents one of the topics that public officials tend to ignore. However, when comparing the predicted categories with each other, the odds of being refused rather than being provided with information are higher for almost all topics (except topic 11). Overall, examining the models' odds ratios provides preliminary support for Hypothesis 1. Responsiveness is found to be significantly dependent on the specific request topic, and requests on a topic of high accountability-seeking potential are more likely to receive a (refusal) response. The odd ratios in Model II finally support this hypothesis.

All others equal, in Model I, a one unit increase in communication tone multiplies by 1.12 (1.29) the odds of receiving a refusal response (a successful response) rather than no response at all. This implies that it increases the odds by 12% (29%). A unit increase refers to a step up in the measurement scale of the underlying numeric value of the communication tone, which consequently outlines more positive words in the request's text. A five-unit increase in communication tone would multiply the odds of receiving a refusal response rather than no response by exp (5*0.1122) = 1.75, increasing the odds by 75%. Hence, this model backs the assumption that the more positive a request is formulated, the higher the odds of receiving a response. Whether this response is more likely to be a refusal or a successful information provision can be approached by comparing the odds ratios between the two outcome categories. Holding all else equal, a request with an identical tone of communication is 1.15 (1.29/1.12) times more likely to be successfully responded to than to be refused, compared to requests with lower levels of communication tone. The length of the request text also affects responsiveness. Longer texts are slightly more likely to be ignored. In addition, we perform robustness checks. First, we conduct an exploratory analysis to show whether the communication tone can influence or redirect responsiveness to certain topics over others. Tone thus acts as a moderator, meaning that the degree of positivity in the tone can trigger the likeliness of receiving a successful answer to topics that are usually rather neglected by authorities. Plots of selected high, low and neutral values of the communication tone in Figure 1 illustrate the underlying relations. While requests with neutral or positive tone show high probability of being successfully processed across all topics, requests with a negative tone are very likely to be ignored. However, apart from the already outlined findings in favor of both hypotheses, no substantial latent pattern of specific interaction between tone and topic could be observed. Second, Appendix D in Supporting Information S1 summarizes the results of the binary logistic model, testing the effect of request's topic and communication tone on whether the request was resolved or ignored. The results do not substantially deviate from the findings of the main models (Models I and II).

First, we examine descriptive statistics of all numeric predictors and the frequency distributions of the categorical predictors. Table 1 provides the crosstabulation along with the mean values of the independent variables across the outcome categories. In Appendix C in Supporting Information S1, a frequency distribution of the number of requests over the years and across topics is provided.

Statistical analysis of the crosstabulation yields a significant chi-square test of independence (χ 2 = 16,721.29, p < 0.000), rejecting the null hypothesis and outlining an underlying relationship between the categorical topic variables. Overall, the frequency distributions reveal a first hint toward substantiating a topic-responsiveness contingency. While requests about education and public safety and security were mostly (at least partially) successful in relative terms (over 60% of requests about topic 3 and topic 4), the opposite is true for requests about hygiene regulations and consumer protection, of which over 60% remained unanswered (topic 11).

A different picture emerges for the communication tone. The mean values are stable across the predicted variable's categories and close to zero. However, when looking at the distinct ranges between the minimum and maximum values across the predicted categories, some variation becomes apparent. To further investigate differences in communication tone across categories, a one-way ANOVA model (F = 53.32, p < 0.000) was run, confirming within-group differences in communication tone.

Second, the two regression models with exponentiations of the base log-odds coefficients (odd ratios) for all predictors are provided in Table 2. Analyses of the different topics' odds ratio patterns and their values, not only within a predicted category, but also across predicted categories, outline an association between the request's topic and responsiveness (p values below the 5% threshold) in Model I. This association is confirmed by the significant relationship between accountability-seeking potential and responsiveness in Model II. Requests about topics of higher ascribed public relevance have relatively high odds of receiving a response from the organization addressed, regardless of whether the response is a refusal or a successful information provision.

transparency initiatives increase, so does interest in how public organizations handle information demand and how responsive they are to different types of information requests.

In this study, we focused on advancing a human-oriented perspective of responsiveness (Saltzstein, 1985(Saltzstein, , 1992) by investigating whether the request's topic and communication tone are decisive for obtaining access to public information. To investigate these effects, information requests posted on a German online FOI platform were analyzed. Multiple text mining procedures were used to assign latent topics and emotions to each request, and findings from a multinomial logistic regression model confirmed both previously established hypotheses.

First, the analysis of over 100,000 requests for government information provides an overview of the topics that citizens are interested in. Whereas previous research focused on the government's drivers for opening up (Mabillard & Keuffer, 2022;Tavares & da Cruz, 2020;Wang & Lo, 2016;Grimmelikhuijsen & Feeney, 2017), this study sheds light on the demand side of open government by investigating which topics are most relevant for citizens. Data on hygiene regulations and consumer protection were requested most often, which might have been due to the campaign initiated by the platform. It seems that citizens are more interested in local issues that can have a direct and immediate effect on them such as non-compliance with hygiene regulations and resulting potential harms to consumers' health. This is substantiated by the high demand for information on public health topics such as questions on public health policies, especially on the COVID-19 crisis. In times of crisis, citizens seem to look for ways to contact government to get information on topics they might be concerned about. Likewise, many requests have been made about individual needs, which points to some degree of public misuse of FOI platforms for the purpose of acquiring mere administrative information on how to access specific public aid or personal benefits. In addition, the public demand for information on correspondence between public employees, their spending behavior, meetings with stakeholder groups and lobbyists as well as about legislation and public contracts illustrates the public's interest in uncovering corruption or questionable government practices.

Second, we linked the demand side of open government use to the supply side by analyzing public organizations' responses to citizen information requests (Nikiforova & Lnenicka, 2021). Drawing on principles of accountability (Berliner et al., 2018;Busuioc & Lodge, 2016, 2017), we argued that responding to information requests may depend on the specific information demanded. Our findings back this claim and are in line with prior research indicating variations in bureaucratic responsiveness to citizens' requests depending on the specific information requested (Bagozzi et al., 2021;Berliner et al., 2021;Ruijer et al., 2020).

On the one hand, topics with the potential to raise public salience, such as government secrecy, public spending, and safety and security, receive more bureaucratic attention. In these cases, even if the requested information is likely to be refused, the request will at least receive a response. Public officials may be disproportionately focused on responding to such requests due to reputational concerns (Busuioc & Lodge, 2017).

On the other hand, public organizations are less likely to respond when citizens ask for low accountability-seeking topics -topics of individual needs and private issues. Such requests about rather unobtrusive, micro-level interests that may be important only for the individual requester are more likely to be ignored. Since low accountability-seeking requests are only important for a small audience, reputational concerns of public officials for being unresponsive might remain marginal (Busuioc & Lodge, 2017). Public organizations thus seem to act strategically and respond to citizen requests if the benefits greatly outweigh the costs. Such difference in responsiveness represents a form of administrative discrimination. This, however, can have serious consequences for the organizations, as citizens expect to be taken seriously and to receive a response from public bodies (Ansell & Miura, 2020;Thomas, 2013). By requesting information citizens exercise control and thus make use of the key democratic ideal of mutual checks and balances. Sartori (1987) emphasizes the importance of 'quality' in democracy, arguing that a well-functioning and effective political system can be achieved, among other things, through the use of checks and balances and the existence of an informed and engaged citizenry. If requests are left unanswered, expectations are not met and checks are not fulfilled. The resulting lack of control can lead to mistrust, a lack of public accountability and citizens' unwillingness to interact with government. In line with these democratic concepts, citizens' expectations of responsiveness and adequate information provision create accountability pressures within public organizations.

Third, citizens' emotions, conveyed by the communication tone, influence the response to the request. Based on previous literature on online communication behavior (Anderson et al., 2014;Epstein et al., 2021;Stieglitz & Dang-Xuan, 2013;Tromble, 2018), underlying emotions affect interpersonal interaction. The way in which a message is perceived by the receiver can significantly influence the response. While Cuillier (2010) or Epstein et al. (2021) found that threatening and angry messages, respectively, receive faster responses from public officials than more friendly ones, this research delves deeper and shows that the underlying emotions a request evokes can be associated with responsiveness. Requests that evoke more positive sentiments are more likely not only to receive a response from a public organization in general, but also to receive the information requested. This outcome sheds light on the human aspect and the role of emotions in citizen-government encounters. Although public officials work on behalf of citizens and disclosure should be guided by FOI laws, this study shows that officials' responsiveness is susceptible to underlying emotions through the words used and the communication tone.

sections.paper_id: Trautendorfer et al. - 2024 - Are the answers all out there Investigating citizen information requests in the haze of bureaucrati
paragraphs: The study shows that responsiveness to citizen information requests is not simply determined by the institutional environment or FOI regulations. A request's topic and communication tone contribute to the response patterns of public organizations. The citizen-driven model of bureaucratic responsiveness explains this phenomenon: in addition to rules and regulations, the underlying emotions of the actors involved in the information exchange process must also be taken into consideration. On the one hand, positive underlying emotions can be responsible for the overall higher likelihood of bureaucratic responsiveness, while on the other hand, the requests' accountability-seeking potential may also trigger responsiveness. However, responsiveness does not mean that information is provided equally. Especially sensitive, high-accountability seeking topics have a high probability of receiving responses, but these responses tend to be refusals of information.

The results provide some recommendations for citizens when requesting access to public information. If citizens want to increase the odds of receiving a response to their request, they are advised to emphasize their request as highly salient, be careful how they phrase their request, and focus on a positive tone of communication, including also polite phrases and general courtesy. The emotion that a request evokes may influence the way it is responded to. At the same time, this study has important implications for public organizations by providing a structured overview of topics that citizens are interested in. When promoting transparency, resource scarcity is mentioned as one of the main barriers to disclosing government information (Gil-Garcia & Sayogo, 2016;Grimmelikhuijsen & Feeney, 2017). However, knowing which topics are of interest to citizens could help governments in setting priorities related to transparency.

Some study limitations are worth noting. First, while this study establishes a link between citizen information requests and the official response to those requests, it was not able to draw any conclusions about how citizens reacted to the response they received. Future research is recommended on the consequences of bureaucratic responsiveness or information refusal, and how citizens and society react to limited government openness. Ignoring citizens' requests could influence citizens' perceptions of government and their future participation behavior.

Second, bureaucratic responsiveness was operationalized according to the public officials' response provided through the platform. Since this study did not analyze whether their responses complied with the FOI laws, a more detailed examination of government responses would be a pertinent research area. In this context, it would also be insightful to examine how the distinctive laws work and to what extent these laws influence street-level bureaucrats' decision-making and their compliance with FOI in Germany (as suggested in Holsen and Pasquier (2012) and Mabillard and Keuffer (2022)). The different legal elements of the FOI laws could be extracted and analyzed whether stricter regulations are met with higher compliance (similar to Wagner, 2021), thereby shedding light on the relationship between de jure and de facto transparency. This could be useful for policy makers and future evaluations of transparency policies.

Third, we only analyzed requests submitted through the platform, and did not consider information requested through other means of submitting requests (mail, email, social media channels), which might have led to a bias in the demand for different topics. The channel might influence the type of information requested by citizens, for example, due to socio-demographic differences in the requesters themselves and in the topics of interest to different requesters. Moreover, some requesters might face barriers to using online platforms due to limited technological expertize or simply be unaware of the existence of FOI platforms.
